title: Bṛhannamaskāra — Vajra-Pañjara Stotra (First Smaraṇa)
summary: This is the first of the Nine Smaraṇas, the protective hymns recited in the fortnightly Pākṣika pratikramaṇa. It takes the great Namaskāra (Navkāra) mantra — the salutation to the five Supreme Beings — and turns it into a 'diamond cage' (vajra-pañjara) of protection: verse by verse the reciter mentally places each line of the mantra over a part of the body and around themselves, building an impenetrable spiritual armour and fortress. It is recited for safety, and the closing verse promises that one who keeps this protection daily is never overcome by fear, disease, or mental affliction.
ritualPlacement: It opens the block of Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa) inside the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa, after the Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. The Smaraṇas belong to the Pākṣika layer (they are not part of the daily Devasi/Rāi). By the book's own substitution rule, the Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī pratikramaṇas have no independent text — they reuse the entire Pākṣika sequence (this Smaraṇa included), replacing the word 'pakkhī' with 'cāumāsī' or 'saṁvatsarī' and lengthening the kāyotsarga; so this stotra recurs there by reference, never as separate text.
authorAndSource: The book attributes it to the 'pūrva-sūris' (the former ācāryas) and does not name a single composer — verse 7 says the protection was 'kathitā pūrvasūribhiḥ' (told by the earlier preceptors). Its doctrinal core is the Pañca-Namaskāra / Navkāra mantra (the nine padas), which it embeds line by line; the framing verses are in anuṣṭubh metre (the heading reads 'anuṣṭup-vṛttaṁ'). It is popularly known as the Vajra-Pañjara Stotra ('diamond-cage hymn'). A specific named author beyond 'pūrva-sūris' is not attested in this book and is left unstated.
structureArc: Verse 1 is the saṅkalpa (resolve): the reciter declares that he remembers the Parameṣṭhi-namaskāra, 'the essence, made of nine padas', as a self-guarding diamond cage. Verses 2–6 are the armouring proper, mapping each line of the Navkāra onto a piece of defensive gear or fortification: v2 — 'Namo Arihantāṇaṁ' as the helmet on the head, 'Namo Siddhāṇaṁ' as the fine cloth over the mouth; v3 — 'Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ' as the body-armour, 'Namo Uvajjhāyāṇaṁ' as the firm weapon in both hands; v4 — 'Namo Loe Savvasāhūṇaṁ' as the auspicious foot-guards, 'Eso Pañca Namukkāro' as the diamond slab underfoot; v5 — 'Savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo' as the diamond rampart (vapra) outside, 'Maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ' as the moat of glowing khadira-coals (khātikā); v6 — the svāhā-sealed, foremost-auspicious pada as the diamond lid (pidhāna) roofing the fortress, completing the enclosure. Verse 7 praises this mighty protection as the destroyer of petty calamities, sprung from the Parameṣṭhi-padas and taught by the ancient sūris. Verse 8 is the phalaśruti: whoever thus makes the protection by the Parameṣṭhi-padas always has neither fear, nor bodily disease, nor mental distress.
recurringImagery: One sustained image runs through the whole hymn: the reciter as a warrior sealed inside a fortified, armoured 'diamond cage'. Each pada of the Navkāra mantra becomes one element of this defence, moving from the body outward and from top to bottom, then sealing the enclosure: head-helmet → mouth-cloth → torso-armour → hand-weapon → foot-guards → diamond floor-slab → outer diamond rampart (vapra) → fiery moat (khātikā) → diamond lid (pidhāna) overhead. Because the imagery is built cumulatively across vv.2–6, the per-verse commentaries need only name which fortress-element a given line supplies; the overall scheme — Navkāra-pada equals fortification, body within, fortress without, all of 'vajra' (adamantine) substance — is established once, here.
keyThemes: the Pañca-Parameṣṭhi and the Navkāra mantra
the body and self sealed in adamantine armour and fortress
removal of fear, disease, and mental affliction

glossary.definition: 'Diamond cage' — the adamantine, impenetrable protective enclosure the reciter mentally builds; the hymn's title image.
The five Supreme Beings — arihanta, siddha, ācārya, upādhyāya, sādhu — saluted in the Navkāra and invoked here for protection.
Reverent salutation; here the Pañca-Namaskāra / Navkāra mantra whose lines are placed over the body.
The 'nine padas' — the five salutation-lines plus the four concluding lines (Eso pañca…, Savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo…, Maṅgalāṇaṁ…, Paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ) that make up the full Navkāra.
The 'foe-destroyer' — a Tīrthaṅkara/omniscient who has conquered the inner enemies; first of the Parameṣṭhins.
A fully liberated, bodiless soul; second of the Parameṣṭhins.
Head preceptor of the order; third of the Parameṣṭhins.
Teaching-preceptor who imparts the scriptures; fourth of the Parameṣṭhins.
An ascetic monk; the 'all sādhus of the world' form the fifth Parameṣṭhin category.
Auspiciousness / blessing; the Navkāra is declared the 'foremost auspicious thing' (paḍhamaṁ maṅgalaṁ).
Rampart or fortress wall; in v5 the 'savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo' line becomes the outer adamantine rampart.
A defensive moat or trench; here a moat of glowing khadira (acacia) embers encircling the fortress.
A lid or covering; the diamond roof sealing the fortress from above (v6).
The ritual sealing-exclamation; the 'svāhā-ending pada' caps and empowers the protective formula.
A 'remembrance' — a protective stotra meant to be recalled/recited; this is the first of the nine.
The closing 'statement of fruit' (v8) declaring the reward of the recitation — freedom from fear, disease, and affliction.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply follow the picture: each verse takes one line of the familiar Navkāra mantra and turns it into a piece of armour or a fortress wall around you, ending with a promise of safety — read it as a guided visualization of protection. An advanced practitioner will recognize the deliberate Navkāra-pada-to-fortification mapping and the anuṣṭubh framing, and can dwell on each Parameṣṭhi's placement as a meditative nyāsa.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text, placement of the stotra as the 1st of the Nine Smaraṇas in the Pākṣika, and the 'pūrva-sūris' attribution (v7).

sources.title: Navkāra (Pañca-Namaskāra) Mantra — standard Jain liturgical text
sources.consultedFor: Identification and meaning of the nine padas embedded line-by-line in the stotra.

notes: The book names no individual author, only the 'pūrva-sūris' (former preceptors), and this preface does not assert one. The popular title 'Vajra-Pañjara Stotra' is widely used for this text but is not given as a heading in the book beyond 'વજ પંજર સ્તોત્ર' at v1. The verse text is mixed Prakrit (the Navkāra lines) and Sanskrit (the framing), preserved verbatim including the book's orthography (e.g. 'વજ' for vajra); mātrās, anusvāras, and conjuncts follow the book. External web sources confirmed the general meaning of the Navkāra and the 'diamond-cage' protective concept but did not yield a single verifiable named composer, so authorship is left as 'pūrva-sūris'.
